Idiom (Supyire): Jwooni mii ɲááŋ, li si fijà li cyiinni te mi.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed gnomic clause with concessive adjective phrase plus negative verbal predicate; proverb-like S–object–verb-final construction.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Even a very capable person or tool cannot solve every problem alone, especially when self-help has reached its limit.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: A person needs help from others for some tasks.
Usage Contexts: teamwork, apprenticeship, leadership, farming cooperation, conflict mediation, teaching
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Linked to documented Supyire proverb tradition around craft knowledge and mutual help; especially meaningful in village work where tools, elders, and kinship support each other.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Supyire): /ɟwóːnì míː ɲáːŋ | lì sí fìɟà lì cjìːnnì té mí/
First Example Sentence: Yahafoore ku baara na nɔ mɛ, u jwo: «Jwooni mii ɲááŋ, li si fijà li cyiinni te mi.»
Second Example Sentence: Kafeefoo u à pyàbii yyere, bà pi cè na jwooni mii ɲááŋ, li si fijà li cyiinni te mi.
Third Example Sentence: Mu ká mpyi mii tɛ̀gɛ, mii sí mu tɛ̀gɛ, ɲaha na jwooni mii ɲááŋ, li si fijà li cyiinni te mi.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative fixed clause with singular subject, object noun, and final negative marker; compact proverbial statement.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: One person acting alone cannot accomplish what requires collective strength.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: People must cooperate to complete difficult work.
Usage Contexts: family decisions, communal farming, village projects, youth advice, cooperative labor
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects Supyire extended-family values and cooperative field labor; the image of a single finger emphasizes the weakness of isolated effort.
First Example Sentence: Wuu ká ɲìŋke baara pyi, nùmpiiŋi kánha lɔ̀hɔɔli yíri mɛ.
Second Example Sentence: Pi à caanga baga yaha tɛ̀ɛri na, bà pi cè na nùmpiiŋi kánha lɔ̀hɔɔli yíri mɛ.
Third Example Sentence: Kolo mɛ̀ɛr'à jwo pyàbii á: nùmpiiŋi kánha lɔ̀hɔɔli yíri mɛ, yii kà fyiin mɛ.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Simple negative declarative with divine subject, object noun phrase, verb, and final negation; theological idiom.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: God is not manipulated by offerings; human ritual must be joined with right conduct and humility.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: A gift or sacrifice alone does not force God to act.
Usage Contexts: religion, moral teaching, ritual discussion, elders’ counsel, theological translation
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Rooted in Supyire religious discourse where God is invoked often but is also understood as beyond ordinary human transactions.
First Example Sentence: Sáraga ku pyi na, kolo u à jwo na Kile taanri ti cɛri mɛ.
Second Example Sentence: Shin ká kapege pyi na, u kà jwumpe tɛ̀ɛri ná yaayi pyi, ɲaha na Kile taanri ti cɛri mɛ.
Third Example Sentence: Mii à u tɛ̀gɛ: mu cè na Kile taanri ti cɛri mɛ, mu funŋɔ pyi sùpyigire na.

Idiom (Supyire): Kile sí sahanŋi fwo mu ɲwɔhɔ na.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Blessing formula with future/modal auxiliary, transitive verb, object noun, and benefactive/locative phrase.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: May the road ahead be safe, open, and free of trouble.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Travel safely and may your journey go well.
Usage Contexts: farewells, travel, migration, market trips, family blessings
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: A travel blessing tied to paths between villages, markets, fields, and family compounds; the road is imagined as something God can clear.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Supyire): /kìlé sí sáhàŋì fwó mú ɲwɔ́hɔ̀ ná/
First Example Sentence: Mu ká Koutiala caanga sa, Kile sí sahanŋi fwo mu ɲwɔhɔ na.
Second Example Sentence: Ceewe u à u pyà yyere: Kile sí sahanŋi fwo mu ɲwɔhɔ na, mu pa bɛɛ na.
Third Example Sentence: Wuu à ɲahapyiifoore kànha yyere, bà Kile sí sahanŋi fwo uru ɲwɔhɔ na.

Idiom (Supyire): Kile sí naamu nɔ pyà á.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Blessing formula with divine subject, future/modal auxiliary, causative-like verb phrase, and dative child object.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: May the child thrive, be nourished, and grow under protection.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: May the baby grow well and be healthy.
Usage Contexts: birth, family visits, mother-child care, naming events, household blessings
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Associated with birth congratulations and maternal care; milk symbolizes life, sweetness, and a child’s first security.
First Example Sentence: Pyà ku nɔ na, pi à jwo: Kile sí naamu nɔ pyà á.
Second Example Sentence: Naa u à pyà yiri, kolo u à u yyere na Kile sí naamu nɔ pyà á.
Third Example Sentence: Baga funŋke e, wuu puni jwo: Kile sí naamu nɔ pyà á, u yaha bɛɛ na.

Idiom (Supyire): Kile sí niɲyahawa yaha kuru cyage e.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Formulaic blessing with divine subject, future/modal auxiliary, object of abundance, and locative complement.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: May what was given, lost, or spent be restored with abundance.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: May you receive much in return.
Usage Contexts: gift exchange, condolence, sacrifice, gratitude, hospitality, repayment
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Used around reciprocity and restoration; it reflects the moral economy of gifts, hospitality, and communal obligation.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Supyire): /kìlé sí nìɲjáhàwà jáhà kùɾù cjáɡè é/
First Example Sentence: Mu à niŋkanya kan mii á; Kile sí niɲyahawa yaha kuru cyage e.
Second Example Sentence: Wuu à kànya pi á, pi à wuu yyere na Kile sí niɲyahawa yaha kuru cyage e.
Third Example Sentence: Cɛ̀ɛyɛ ku à furu ta, kolo u jwo: Kile sí niɲyahawa yaha kuru cyage e.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Short blessing clause with divine subject, future/modal auxiliary, and transitive verb; elliptical night-farewell formula.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: May morning come peacefully after the night.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Sleep well; may tomorrow arrive safely.
Usage Contexts: goodnight greetings, family speech, child care, travel lodging, elder blessings
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Night and morning mark safety, vulnerability, and continuity in village life; the phrase turns a simple farewell into a protective blessing.
First Example Sentence: Tèni ku à ta, naa u jwo pyàbii á: Kile sí sɔ̀gɔ jya.
Second Example Sentence: Kolo u à wuu yyere canŋa pye na, u jwo: Kile sí sɔ̀gɔ jya.
Third Example Sentence: Mu yaha bɛɛ na, Kile sí sɔ̀gɔ jya, wuu sí jwo sɔ̀gɔ na.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative habitual clause with locative/community subject metaphor, verb of silence/rest, and final negation.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: News, gossip, and public opinion spread quickly in a market or crowded public place.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: People talk a lot in public places.
Usage Contexts: market life, gossip, politics, trade, warnings, humor
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Draws on Koutiala-area market culture, where trading, bargaining, news, jokes, and public judgment circulate together.
First Example Sentence: Mu kà jwumpe kɛ̀ɛri caanga na mɛ, caanga ɲwɔhɔ kà yyêre mɛ.
Second Example Sentence: Aminata à yiri fànha na, lire ɲyɛ na caanga ɲwɔhɔ kà yyêre mɛ.
Third Example Sentence: Pi à sàri kànha kan, ŋka caanga ɲwɔhɔ kà yyêre mɛ, jwumpe pɛ̀ɛ na.

Idiom (Supyire): Baga funŋɔ tɛɛri, pyàbii sí yaha tɛɛri.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Conditional/parallel proverb-like clause with household metaphor and plural subject; balanced coordination.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: When the household is united, children and dependents are also guided in unity.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Family harmony helps everyone live well together.
Usage Contexts: family counsel, marriage, inheritance, child upbringing, conflict resolution
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects the Supyire emphasis on extended family solidarity: working together, eating together, listening to one another, and following shared customs.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Supyire): /báɡà fúŋɔ̀ tɛ́ːɾí | pjâbíː sí jáhà tɛ́ːɾí/
First Example Sentence: Kolo u à pìlaga yyere, baga funŋɔ tɛɛri, pyàbii sí yaha tɛɛri.
Second Example Sentence: Ceewe ná cɛ̀ɛyɛ pi à jwumpe tɛ̀ɛri, bà baga funŋɔ tɛɛri, pyàbii sí yaha tɛɛri.
Third Example Sentence: Mii à u jwo na baga funŋɔ tɛɛri, pyàbii sí yaha tɛɛri, ɲaha na fyiin ɲyɛ funŋke e.

Idiom (Supyire): Sùpyire ɲwɔhɔ sí jwumpe kolo yaha.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Metalinguistic idiom with people/language subject, future/habitual auxiliary, speech noun, and verb of leaving/placing.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: The words of elders or the community preserve memory, judgment, and identity beyond the present moment.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Oral tradition keeps important knowledge alive.
Usage Contexts: storytelling, elder speech, teaching, history, literature, cultural preservation
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Connected to Supyire oral literature, storytelling, blessings, proverbs, and ongoing lexicography/text-archive work to preserve language and culture.
First Example Sentence: Kolo u ká jwo, sùpyire ɲwɔhɔ sí jwumpe kolo yaha, pyàbii pi sí cè.
Second Example Sentence: Wuu ká yìri kɛ̀ɛrɛ na, wuu kà jwumpe yaha mɛ, ɲaha na sùpyire ɲwɔhɔ sí jwumpe kolo yaha.
Third Example Sentence: Farakala shiinbii pi à yyege jwo, bà sùpyire ɲwɔhɔ sí jwumpe kolo yaha.
